Ansible inventory: How would you structu: Use Ansible's dynamic inventory plugins (gcp_compute, aws_ec2, azure_rm) configured with tag-based or label-based grouping, so hosts are discovered live from each cloud API instead of hand-maintained in an INI file that drifts from reality. Cache inventory results with a short TTL to avoid hammering cloud APIs on every run, and group hosts by environment/role tags so playbooks can target `env_prod:&role_web` style selectors consistently across clouds.
Ansible error handling: How do you handl: By default Ansible removes a failed host from the current play but continues on the rest of the fleet; use `max_fail_percentage` or `any_errors_fatal: false` to control how much failure is tolerated before aborting the whole run, and `ignore_errors: true` sparingly for genuinely non-critical tasks. Always review the retry file Ansible generates (`.retry`) or a summary report at the end so failed hosts are remediated deliberately rather than silently skipped.
Ansible Jinja2 templating: How would you: Use the `template` module with `.j2` files that reference group_vars/host_vars for environment-specific values, keeping secrets out of templates by referencing Ansible Vault-encrypted variables instead of hardcoding them. Validate rendered output with `--check --diff` before applying, and use Jinja2 filters and default() guards defensively so a missing variable fails loudly in CI rather than rendering a broken config silently in production.
Ansible Tower/AWX: What does Ansible Tow: Tower/AWX adds a web UI and REST API for launching and scheduling playbook runs, RBAC so only authorized teams can run specific job templates against specific inventories, credential management that keeps SSH keys and secrets out of individual laptops, and a full audit trail of who ran what, when, and with what result. It also supports workflow templates that chain multiple playbooks with approval gates, which plain ansible-playbook has no concept of.
Ansible OS patching: How would you desig: Use `serial:` in the playbook to patch hosts in small rolling batches (e.g. 10% at a time) with a health check task after each batch that verifies the service is healthy before proceeding to the next batch. Snapshot or take a backup before patching so a bad patch can be rolled back, and gate the run behind a pre-check task that confirms the host is drained from the load balancer first so patching does not cause user-facing errors.
Ansible linting: How would you enforce A: Run ansible-lint and yamllint as a pre-commit hook and again in CI so style and common-mistake rules (missing handlers, hardcoded credentials, deprecated syntax) are caught before merge, not at run time against production. Require a syntax-check and `--check --diff` dry-run in the PR pipeline, and mandate human review for any playbook touching production inventory groups, with the diff clearly showing which hosts and tasks will be affected.
Ansible secrets in CI/CD: How would you: Store the Vault password and SSH private key in the CI system's secret store (GitHub Actions secrets, GitLab CI variables) and inject them at runtime as masked environment variables or a temporary file created only for the job's duration, never committed to the repo. Prefer a Vault password file supplied via `--vault-password-file` pointing at a runtime-generated path, and rotate the Vault password and keys periodically, especially after any CI credential exposure.
Ansible multi-cloud: You delivered Ansib: Each cloud needed its own authentication mechanism - GCP via a service account JSON key or Workload Identity, AWS via IAM role/access keys, Azure via a service principal - configured through separate collections (google.cloud, amazon.aws, azure.azcollection) with cloud-specific module naming and required parameters. Abstracted common patterns (tagging, naming conventions, network setup) into shared roles where possible, but kept cloud-specific modules and variable files separate since resource models and required fields differ significantly between providers.
Ansible with Kubernetes: When would you: Ansible remains useful for bootstrapping the underlying VMs before a cluster exists (OS hardening, kubelet/container runtime install, joining nodes), managing infrastructure that is not containerized (bastion hosts, on-prem servers, network appliances), and one-off operational tasks like credential rotation or OS patching across a fleet. Once workloads themselves are containerized, Kubernetes-native tools (Helm, Kustomize, operators) take over application lifecycle management, with Ansible handling the layer below the cluster.
Ansible change management: How would you: Classify playbooks by blast radius: routine, well-tested changes (config drift fixes, minor updates) run automatically via CI/CD on merge, while high-risk changes (fleet-wide restarts, OS upgrades, security group changes) require a manual approval gate in the pipeline or an AWX workflow approval node before execution. Always run `--check --diff` first so approvers see exactly what will change, and require the change to go through a small rolling batch (serial:) even after approval so a bad change is caught on a subset of hosts before it reaches the whole fleet.
Scenario - partial playbook failure: An: First check what state hosts 1-39 were left in versus 40-200 by reviewing the task that failed and whether it was transactional (fully applied or not) or could have partially applied on host 40 itself. Use the auto-generated `.retry` file or a targeted `--limit @failed_hosts.retry` run to retry only the affected and remaining hosts once the root cause (a bad package version, unreachable dependency, etc.) is fixed, rather than re-running against all 200 and risking side effects on the hosts that already succeeded. Add a health-check task and smaller `serial:` batches so a future failure is caught after 5 hosts instead of 40.
Scenario - vaulted secret rotation: You: Add the new secret value alongside the old one first (many systems support dual-active credentials briefly) so services can pick up the new secret without a hard cutover, update the Vault-encrypted variable and roll it out via a rolling `serial:` batch during a low-traffic window, and verify each batch's health before proceeding. Only revoke the old secret once every environment confirms it's using the new one, and keep the old value available for a short rollback window in case a missed host or cached credential surfaces after rollout.
